comments:
Summary and contributions:
The paper presents a novel, general framework linking MAP estimation in graphical models, linear-programming relaxations, and message-passing algorithms to the combinatorial notion of perfect graphs. The author shows how any discrete graphical model can be converted to a nand Markov random field (NMRF) whose MAP problem is a set-packing LP. Using classical results about integrality of set-packing LPs and modern results on perfect graphs (including the strong perfect graph theorem and polynomial recognition algorithms), the paper characterizes when the LP relaxation is integral (hence MAP is in P) and consequently when certain convergent message-passing algorithms recover the exact MAP. The paper also gives pruning procedures (Disconnect and Merge) to simplify the NMRF and provides some experiments on matching-related graph families.

Strengths:
- The reduction to an NMRF and the connection to set-packing LP integrality is clear and theoretically elegant.
- Leveraging the theory of perfect graphs (including the strong perfect graph theorem and recognition algorithms) provides a precise, general condition for LP integrality and exact MAP recovery.
- The paper connects recent results on convergent message passing to these integrality conditions, thereby widening the class of graphs for which message passing is guaranteed exact.
- Proofs and arguments are generally well structured and reference the appropriate theorems.

Weaknesses and limitations:
- Practicality is a serious concern. The NMRF expansion (Eq.2) can cause an exponential blow-up in the number of binary variables: N is the sum over cliques of the product of constituent variable cardinalities. The paper acknowledges this but gives only limited guidance about when the conversion is feasible in practice.
- Even when N is moderate, the perfect-graph recognition algorithm used (Chudnovsky et al.) has a high polynomial runtime (O(|V|^9)), and the paper notes that, currently, only very small graphs (N < ~20) can be tested practically. This substantially limits immediate applicability.
- Experimental evaluation is limited. The experiments are focused on matching-related families (where the connection to perfect graphs is already known) and on random graphs; broader experiments on other practical graphical models (e.g., vision or NLP instances with moderate clique sizes) and scalability studies are missing.
- Some algorithmic procedures (Disconnect, Merge) are described at a high level; more formal analysis or empirical evidence of their benefit would strengthen the practical side.
- The claim about message passing exactness relies on convergent message passing (which is shown to match LP for binary variables). The conjecture that vanilla max-product might also work on NMRFs with perfect graphs is left open; the paper should avoid overclaiming here.
- Numerical/implementation details are brief (e.g., replacing −∞ by a large negative constant), and handling ties/multiple MAP solutions is noted but not deeply discussed.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Provide more extensive empirical evaluation that illustrates the tradeoffs: size of original model vs. NMRF size, effect of Disconnect/Merge on N, performance of message passing vs. LP vs. exact combinatorial solvers on a variety of models.
- Discuss heuristics or sufficient conditions on the original graphical model structure that keep the NMRF size reasonable (so the method can be used in practice), and give guidelines for practitioners.
- Include clearer pseudocode for conversion and pruning, and perhaps complexity analysis that emphasizes which problem instances are tractable.
- Clarify handling of numerical stability and ties in MAP solutions and in message passing/LP solvers.

Overall assessment:
This paper makes a strong theoretical contribution by exposing a clean connection between MAP integrality and perfect graph structure via the NMRF reduction. It broadens the family of problems for which LP and message-passing exactness can be guaranteed. However, practical applicability is limited by potential exponential blow-up in model size and by the high cost of perfect-graph recognition. The work is conceptually significant and of interest to researchers working on inference, LP relaxations, and combinatorial optimization; with additional empirical and practical algorithmic development it could have broader impact.
